question: [File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send GET /api/v1/blocks with no authentication header, causing RecordFileService.getBlocks() to execute and return full block records including hash, prevHash, count, and logsBloom for every block in the chain, enabling the attacker to reconstruct transaction history and identify signable transaction windows without any owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getByHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user call GET /api/v1/blocks/{hashOrNumber} with no credentials, bypassing all signer/owner authorization checks because validateReq(req) is called with no acceptedParameters set (defaults to emptySet), allowing full block metadata retrieval that exposes consensus timestamps used to authorize transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validateHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a partial hash prefix (e.g., a 64-hex-char string) to validateHashOrNumber() which passes isValidEthHashOrHederaHash(), causing RecordFileService.getByHashOrNumber() to execute a LIKE query with hash+'%' that matches the first block whose hash starts with that prefix, potentially returning a different block than intended and exposing its transaction authorization data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: extractLimitFromFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the string-vs-number type coercion in the comparison limit.value > maxLimit, where limit.value is a raw string from req.query and maxLimit is a number, causing the comparison to behave unexpectedly (e.g., '9' > 10 is false in JS but '99' > 10 is true), allowing the attacker to retrieve more block records than permitted and enumerate transaction authorization data at scale?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: extractOrderFromFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an order parameter value that after toUpperCase() does not match any key in orderFilterValues (e.g., order=DESC; injected), causing orderFilterValues[order.value.toUpperCase()] to return undefined, which is then directly interpolated into the SQL ORDER BY clause in RecordFileService.getBlocks(), resulting in a malformed or injected SQL query that bypasses intended data access controls?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: extractSqlFromBlockFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request where filters.orderBy resolves to a value not sanitized before being directly string-interpolated into the SQL query in RecordFileService.getBlocks() as 'order by ${filters.orderBy} ${filters.order}', enabling SQL injection that could expose or manipulate transaction records without signer approval?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send repeated GET /api/v1/blocks requests with timestamp=gte:0 to enumerate all blocks and their consensus timestamps, then use those timestamps to identify and replay transaction authorization windows without any owner or signer involvement?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: generateNextLink()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the generateNextLink() function which uses last(blocks).index to construct the next pagination link, by manipulating the block.number filter to cause the pagination cursor to skip authorization-sensitive blocks, effectively hiding transaction records from audit trails while still accessing them?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getByHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user pass block number 0 (zero) to getByHashOrNumber() since isPositiveLong(hashOrNumber, true) allows zero with allowZero=true, retrieving the genesis block's hash and consensus timestamps that could be used to forge or replay early transaction authorizations?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: extractSqlFromBlockFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user submit multiple block.number filter parameters (e.g., block.number=gte:0&block.number=lte:999999999) causing extractSqlFromBlockFilters() to build a whereQuery with multiple conditions that together bypass intended range restrictions, returning all blocks and their transaction authorization metadata?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any rate limiting or authentication in getBlocks() to perform high-volume enumeration of all block hashes and prevHash values, reconstructing the full chain of transaction authorizations without any signer or owner consent?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: validateHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a 96-hex-character Hedera hash to validateHashOrNumber() which passes isValidEthHashOrHederaHash(), then have it stripped of '0x' prefix and used in a LIKE query, where the prefix match could collide with a different block's hash, returning unauthorized block data and its associated transaction authorization records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getByHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user call getByHashOrNumber() with a hash that after lowercasing and appending '%' matches multiple blocks in the database, but since getSingleRow() returns only the first match, the attacker can probe different hash prefixes to enumerate block data and discover transaction authorization timestamps without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: extractLimitFromFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user set limit to a value equal to maxLimit (boundary condition) and exploit the strict greater-than check (limit.value > maxLimit) to retrieve exactly maxLimit records in a single request, maximizing data exfiltration of block transaction authorization metadata per request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getBlocks() passes filters.order directly into the SQL query string without parameterization in RecordFileService, and if the order value is not properly constrained to 'asc'/'desc', inject SQL fragments that alter query semantics to expose unauthorized transaction records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: extractOrderByFromFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with both block.number and timestamp filters, causing extractOrderByFromFilters() to select only the first matching filter's column via [0] array access, potentially causing the ORDER BY to use an unexpected column that changes which blocks are returned and which transaction authorization data is exposed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the direct string interpolation of filters.limit into the SQL LIMIT clause in RecordFileService.getBlocks() ('limit ${filters.limit}'), where if filters.limit is not cast to an integer, a crafted string value could inject SQL and bypass row-count restrictions on transaction authorization data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getByHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validateReq(req) in getByHashOrNumber() uses an empty acceptedParameters set, meaning all query parameters are silently ignored rather than rejected, allowing the attacker to append arbitrary parameters that might be interpreted by upstream middleware or proxies to bypass authorization checks?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: generateNextLink()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the pagination link generated by generateNextLink() by crafting a request where blocks.length equals filters.limit, causing the next link to always be generated and enabling infinite pagination through all block records, exposing all transaction authorization data without any access control?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with order=asc and block.number=gte:0 to retrieve blocks in ascending order starting from genesis, using the returned hash and prevHash chain to verify transaction ordering and forge authorization proofs without signer involvement?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: extractSqlFromBlockFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with no filters (empty query), causing extractSqlFromBlockFilters() to return an empty whereQuery array, which causes buildWhereSqlStatement() to produce no WHERE clause, resulting in a full table scan of all record files and exposing all transaction authorization metadata?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getByHashOrNumber()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the hash.toLowerCase() normalization in RecordFileService.getByHashOrNumber() combined with the LIKE prefix match to perform a timing-based oracle attack, probing different hash prefixes to enumerate valid block hashes and reconstruct transaction authorization chains without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the BlockViewModel constructor which exposes logsBloom via toHexString(recordFile.logsBloom, true, 512), leaking EVM event log bloom filter data that reveals which contract addresses and topics were involved in transactions, enabling the attacker to identify and target specific transaction authorization flows?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getBlocks()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the BlockViewModel's exposure of gasUsed (returning null only when gasUsed === -1) to infer transaction complexity and identify high-value blocks containing complex multi-signer transactions, then target those blocks for authorization bypass attempts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/blockController.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: extractOrderFromFilters()] Can an unprivileged external user send multiple order parameters (e.g., order=asc&order=desc), causing findLast() to select the last occurrence, and exploit this last-wins behavior to override any server-side default ordering, controlling which blocks and transaction authorization records are returned first?
